title: Vetting at the Gate
subtitle: Due diligence before you sign

hook.metaphor: You wouldn't hand a stranger the keys to your house without checking who they are, what they'll do inside, and whether they've burgled anyone before. Yet organisations routinely give vendors deep access with barely a glance. **Due diligence** is checking the stranger at the gate *before* you let them in — because afterwards is far too late.
hook.scenario: A team urgently needs a new analytics tool and signs up the cheapest option in an afternoon — handing it live customer data. Nobody checked its security, its data practices, or where the data would be stored. It felt fast and efficient. What did that speed actually buy?

learn: **Due diligence** is assessing a vendor's risk *before* you commit. The cardinal rule: the time to discover a supplier is weak is *before* you depend on them, not after they've leaked your data. Speed bought by skipping the check (our analytics tool) is a loan against a future disaster.

Smart due diligence is **risk-based** — proportionate to how much the vendor could hurt you. A supplier touching sensitive customer data with deep system access (high risk) deserves deep scrutiny; the office stationery supplier doesn't. Tier your vendors so effort goes where the danger is.

What you check depends on the risk, but typically: their **security** posture (certifications like ISO 27001, SOC 2 reports), **financial health** (will they still exist next year?), **data practices** (what will they collect, where will it live, who can see it?), **compliance** record, and any history of breaches or misconduct.

It then gets locked into the **contract** — the gate's legal lock. Good contracts spell out security requirements, data-handling rules, the **right to audit**, breach-notification duties, service levels (SLAs), and exit terms. A handshake and an invoice is not a control.
The principle of **least privilege** (from cyber) applies here too: give each vendor only the access and data they genuinely need — never the master keys when they only need to water the plants.
bridge: So at work, you can insist on a simple discipline before any vendor goes live: *how much could they hurt us (tier the risk), have we actually checked them in proportion, and is it written into the contract with audit rights and least-privilege access?* The afternoon you 'save' skipping due diligence is borrowed from the week you'll lose cleaning up.

interactions.title: The afternoon sign-up
interactions.prompt: A team signs the cheapest analytics tool in an afternoon and hands it live customer data, unchecked. The problem?

options.label: Nothing — fast and cheap is good business.
options.feedback: Fast and cheap, paid for with unchecked exposure of customer data, is a disaster on credit. The speed is real; so is the borrowed risk.

options.label: No risk-based due diligence before handing over sensitive data.
options.feedback: Exactly. A vendor touching live customer data is high-risk and needs proportionate vetting (security, data practices, location) and contractual safeguards before it goes live.

options.label: Fine as long as the tool has a nice interface.
options.feedback: A polished UI tells you nothing about their security or data handling. Looks aren't due diligence.

interactions.title: How much scrutiny?
interactions.prompt: Risk-based due diligence: sort each vendor by the scrutiny it warrants.

items.label: A cloud provider hosting all your customer data
The company that supplies your office coffee
A payroll processor handling staff bank details
A firm with deep admin access to core systems
A vendor printing your branded notebooks

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Vet vendors at the gate — risk-based due diligence on their security, finances, and data practices, locked into a contract with audit rights and least-privilege access — because checking after you depend on them is too late.”

checkpoint.q: Due diligence on a vendor should be done…
options: Before you commit and depend on them
checkpoint.explain: The point is to discover weakness before you rely on them — afterwards is far too late.

options: Scrutinising vendors in proportion to how much they could harm you
checkpoint.explain: Tier vendors by potential impact and spend your effort where the danger is greatest.

checkpoint.q: A strong vendor contract should include…
options: Just a price and a logo
Security requirements, data rules, audit rights, breach notification, and exit terms
checkpoint.explain: The contract is the legal lock on the gate — it must spell out the safeguards and your rights.
